extra.reasoning: The property globally(opted_out implies not sent) holds on every reachable execution. The only transition that sets sent:=true is [t_send_allowed], whose guard is "not opted_out" — so sent can only become true while opted_out is false. The only transition that sets opted_out:=true is [t_optout], and it unconditionally sets sent:=false in the same step, so opting out always clears any pending send. Once opted_out is true, a send_attempt matches only [t_send_blocked] (guard: opted_out), which sets sent:=false; [t_send_allowed] is disabled. The combination-lock machinery (stages 0–10 via key_0/key_1/key_2 with adv_* advances and rst_* resets) is irrelevant to the property: none of those transitions ever set sent:=true or touch opted_out, and in fact every one of them sets sent:=false as a side effect. There is therefore no transition that can establish opted_out=true with sent=true: sent-true co-occurs with opted_out-false (by the send guard), and opted_out-true co-occurs with sent-false (by the optout update). The invariant is preserved by every transition and holds in the initial state (opted_out=false, sent=false), so it is inductively maintained across all executions, including the 13-event bound. No counterexample exists.
